reasoning: Each thread reads 64 floats from array `a` (256 bytes) plus 64 floats from array `b` (256 bytes) and performs 128 FLOPs (64 multiply-adds), then writes 1 float (4 bytes). However, threads within the same warp share the same `base` value (since consecutive thread indices within a group of 64 map to the same base), so the 64 reads from `a[base+j]` are broadcast/cached across threads in the group, but `b[j]` is uniform and small enough to cache. The effective arithmetic intensity is roughly 128 FLOPs / (256 + 4) bytes ≈ 0.49 FLOP/byte for the `a` array reads — far below the ridge point of 62 FLOP/byte. The kernel streams through a large array `a` (256 MB) with each element read by exactly one group of 64 threads, making DRAM bandwidth the dominant limiter. The workload is a classic bandwidth-limited pattern with low arithmetic intensity relative to the bytes moved from global memory.
